questions.question: The but-for test for factual causation asks:

options.A: Whether the defendant owed the plaintiff a duty of care
options.B: But for the defendant's breach, would the plaintiff have suffered the same damage?
options.C: Whether the type of harm was reasonably foreseeable
options.D: Whether a novus actus interveniens broke the chain of causation

questions.explanation: The but-for test is the primary test for factual causation. It asks: but for the defendant's breach of duty, would the plaintiff have suffered the same damage? If the answer is no — the damage would not have occurred but for the breach — causation is established. If yes — the damage would have occurred anyway — causation fails.

questions.question: In Barnett v Chelsea and Kensington Hospital Management Committee [1969], causation failed because:

options.A: The doctor owed no duty of care to the patient
options.B: The patient had contributed to his own condition
options.C: Even if the doctor had examined the patient, nothing could have been done — the patient would have died regardless of the breach
options.D: The arsenic poisoning was too remote a cause to be foreseeable

questions.explanation: In Barnett [1969], the on-call doctor negligently failed to examine a night watchman who had been poisoned by arsenic. The court held causation was not established — even if the doctor had acted non-negligently, the patient would have died. But for the breach, the outcome would have been the same. This is the classic illustration of the but-for test defeating a claim on causation.

questions.question: The Irish Supreme Court case of Maitland v Swan is significant for factual causation because it:

options.A: Abolished the but-for test in favour of the material contribution test
options.B: Confirmed that the but-for test is the starting point for causation analysis in Irish law
options.C: Held that loss of a chance below 50% is recoverable in Ireland
options.D: Applied the Wagon Mound foreseeability test to factual causation

questions.explanation: Maitland v Swan (Irish Supreme Court) confirmed that the but-for test is the starting point for causation analysis. Irish courts apply it rigorously before considering more complex causation scenarios such as material contribution.

questions.question: The but-for test faces its greatest difficulty in which type of scenario?

options.A: Cases involving a single negligent defendant and a single cause of injury
options.B: Cases where the plaintiff has a pre-existing condition
options.C: Multiple causation scenarios where several independent causes each contribute to the harm

questions.explanation: The but-for test breaks down in multiple causation scenarios. For example, where two defendants independently set fires that combine to destroy property, neither fire alone was sufficient — applying the but-for test to each independently would exonerate both, which would be unjust. In response, courts developed the material contribution test.

questions.question: The material contribution test holds that a defendant may be liable if their breach:

options.A: Was the sole cause of the plaintiff's damage
options.B: Materially contributed to the plaintiff's damage, even if it was not the only cause and even if the but-for test cannot be strictly satisfied
options.C: Was a cause of the damage only where all other causes were non-tortious
options.D: Occurred within the limitation period and was foreseeable in type

questions.explanation: The material contribution test was developed to address cases where the but-for test produces an unjust result. A defendant is liable if their breach materially contributed to the plaintiff's damage — even where other causes (tortious or non-tortious) were also operating. It does not require the breach to be the sole or dominant cause.

questions.question: Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956] HoL established the material contribution test on facts involving:

options.A: A road traffic accident with multiple colliding vehicles
options.B: A plaintiff who contracted pneumoconiosis from a combination of tortious and non-tortious silica dust exposure at work
options.C: A patient who lost a 40% chance of recovery due to medical negligence
options.D: Two defendants whose independently negligent acts combined to flood the plaintiff's property

questions.explanation: In Bonnington Castings v Wardlaw [1956], the plaintiff contracted pneumoconiosis from silica dust. Some dust came from a tortious source (negligently maintained equipment) and some from a non-tortious source. The House of Lords held the defendant liable because the tortious exposure materially contributed to the disease — even though its precise contribution could not be quantified.

questions.question: In Philp v Ryan [2004] Irish Supreme Court, the court applied the material contribution test to hold that:

options.A: A hospital was not liable because the patient's pre-existing condition was the dominant cause of harm
options.B: A medical practitioner whose negligence materially contributed to a patient's deterioration could be held liable even where other causative factors were present
options.C: The but-for test was satisfied on the facts because no other cause was operating
options.D: Loss of a chance below 50% was recoverable in Irish law

questions.explanation: Philp v Ryan [2004] is the leading Irish authority on material contribution in a medical context. The Irish Supreme Court confirmed that where a medical practitioner's negligence materially contributed to a patient's deterioration — even alongside other causative factors — the defendant could be held liable. It is the key Irish case to cite alongside Bonnington Castings.

questions.question: In Hotson v East Berkshire Area Health Authority [1987], the House of Lords refused to allow recovery for:

options.A: Pain and suffering resulting from a surgical procedure
options.B: A loss of a less-than-50% chance of a better medical outcome
options.C: Future loss of earnings where the plaintiff had no employment history
options.D: Psychiatric injury caused by witnessing the plaintiff's own physical injury

questions.explanation: In Hotson [1987], the plaintiff argued that the hospital's negligence deprived him of a 25% chance of avoiding a permanent disability. The House of Lords refused recovery — a less-than-50% chance did not satisfy the balance of probabilities standard for causation. The loss of chance doctrine remains contested in Irish law and candidates should note the academic debate.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly distinguishes factual causation from remoteness (legal causation)?

options.A: Factual causation asks whether the breach caused the harm; remoteness asks whether the harm is of a type the law will compensate
options.B: Factual causation applies the Wagon Mound test; remoteness applies the but-for test
options.C: Factual causation is relevant only in personal injury cases; remoteness applies to all tort claims
options.D: There is no practical distinction — both are subsets of the breach inquiry

questions.explanation: Factual causation and remoteness are distinct inquiries that must be kept separate. Factual causation (but-for test / material contribution) asks: did the defendant's breach cause the harm? Remoteness (Wagon Mound) asks: is the harm of a type the law will recognise? A claim can fail on either.

questions.question: On an FE-1 problem question involving causation, the correct analytical sequence is:

options.A: Apply remoteness first, then if satisfied, apply the but-for test
options.B: Apply the but-for test first; if it fails due to multiple causes, consider material contribution; then address remoteness separately
options.C: Apply the material contribution test in all cases as it is the broader and more plaintiff-friendly test
options.D: Apply both tests simultaneously and use whichever produces the better result for the plaintiff

questions.explanation: The correct sequence for causation on the FE-1 is: (1) state and apply the but-for test; (2) if it fails due to multiple causes, move to material contribution (Bonnington Castings; Philp v Ryan); (3) address remoteness separately in Module 5 (Wagon Mound). Always keep factual causation and remoteness as distinct questions.
